messages.content:
YOUR ROLE: You are the partner running a pre-mortem on this market model for a
paying client. The client's own team has already read every number in the
ledger, including every sensitivity band. Your reputation rests on surfacing
what THEY would not list: risks that look benign on the surface but could kill
the venture, argued mechanistically, anchored in the model.

HARD REQUIREMENTS — a hypothesis that misses any of these will be rejected by
a validator before any human reads it:
1. targetNodes: real ledger node ids; perturbation ops only on FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes.
2. mechanism: at least 3 steps (trigger → propagation → model impact), EVERY
   step carrying refs — "ledger:<nodeId>", "digest:<group>#c<n>", or
   "profile:<field>". A step you cannot reference is a step you are guessing.
3. falsifier: the single concrete observation that would kill this risk. If you
   cannot name one, it is not a claim.
4. whyMissable: why a competent founder's own risk list omits this. "It's
   non-obvious" is not an argument; name the surface appearance that hides it.
5. likelihood: a probability with a basis ("evidence" | "base-rate" |
   "judgment") and a rationale. No naked numbers.
6. indicators: at least 2 observable early warnings, each with WHERE to watch
   (a named tender portal, an operator's capex call, a standards docket, a
   vendor's price list) and the reading that should trigger re-assessment.
7. mitigations: at least 2. Where the right move is to buy information, use
   type "information" and name the ledger node it de-risks (voiNodeId) — its
   value will be computed, not asserted.

QUOTAS (per lens): at least one hypothesis self-tagged expectedObviousness
"low", and at least one whose perturbation targets NONE of tamBase /
serviceableFactor / obtainableFactor — the tornado already covers those bands;
look past them.

MINE THE RESIDUALS: the highest-yield material is where sources disagree with
the curated value (the digests' "tensions"), where searches came back empty
(the digests' "absences" — an unquantified market is a risk, not a comfort),
where a derivation's crossCheck diverges from its primary method, and where a
precise-looking value carries maturity "needs-source".

HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

BANNED GENRES (emitting one of these wastes your slot):
- "Find 2 customers to talk to and get an LOI" — an action item, not a risk. It names no node, no mechanism, no falsifier.
- "Do research on customer buying behaviour" — a homework assignment any consultant could write for any company.
- "Competition is strong / the market is concentrated" — the ledger already states CR3. Restating a fact is not a risk; the risk is a MECHANISM that concentration enables.
- "The market may be smaller than expected" — the tamBase band (240–520) already says this. Only worth saying if you name WHICH derivation hop fails and WHY.
- "Regulatory changes could impact the business" — name the instrument, the compliance artifact it requires, and the date it lands, or drop it.
- "The band's low end might be true" with no new reason — the band is already in the model; you are paid for what is NOT in the model.

GOLD EXEMPLAR (the target register — do NOT repeat its content):
{
 "id": "busway-hollowing-hyperscale",
 "title": "Busway + OCP power shelves hollow out the hyperscale 44% from inside the scope boundary",
 "narrative": "The model excludes busbars and power shelves as out-of-scope substitutes — a scoping footnote. But hyperscale is 44% of TAM, and it is precisely the segment whose new AI-density builds are moving power distribution from rack PDUs to overhead busway feeding OCP-style in-rack power shelves. If that architecture becomes the default for new CE hyperscale capacity, the largest slice of this TAM is not 'ours to win slowly' — it is structurally shrinking while the headline number stays flat. The exclusion list quietly became the market thesis.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.hyperscale"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "New hyperscale AI-density builds adopt busway + in-rack DC power shelves instead of discrete rack PDUs",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c?",
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "CE hyperscale capacity additions (the growth the 9% CAGR is made of) carry falling rack-PDU content per MW; replacement demand in legacy halls does not offset it",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:shape.cagr",
    "digest:tam-base#c?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The hyperscale share of the rack-PDU market shrinks in absolute terms — seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates the addressable slice for a new entrant's window",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Substitutes were explicitly considered and excluded — the boundary decision looks handled. The kill path is not 'we misjudged the boundary' but 'the boundary moved into the biggest segment while the TAM base year stayed put'.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "2025–26 CE hyperscale fit-out specs (or Schneider/Vertiv rack-power order mixes) showing intelligent rack PDUs holding share in new AI-density halls.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "OCP power-shelf adoption is documented for AI racks, but CE colocation and enterprise retrofits (56% of TAM) keep buying rack PDUs; timing within a 12-month window is uncertain.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.hyperscale",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.22,
   "note": "≈half of the hyperscale slice migrates to busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack-power architecture specified in new CE hyperscale tenders (busway/shelf vs PDU)",
   "where": "Frankfurt/Amsterdam data-center fit-out tenders; OCP regional summit deployment talks",
   "threshold": "≥2 flagship CE builds specifying shelf-based distribution",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Incumbent rack-PDU revenue mix commentary (Vertiv/Schneider earnings)",
   "where": "Quarterly earnings calls, rack-power product-line commentary",
   "threshold": "PDU line flat while busway line grows double-digit",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE rack-power architecture survey across the 2026 hyperscale build pipeline",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.hyperscale"
  },
  {
   "action": "Qualify the product line for colocation/enterprise retrofit first, where PDU form-factor is sticky",
   "type": "strategic"
  }
 ]
}

content: {"hypotheses":[{"id":"framework-agreement-foreclosure-large-operators","title":"The declared first buyer (large operators, 40% of spend) is closed by global framework agreements, not by competition","narrative":"The venture's stated go-to-market is 'sell to large operators first.' But hyperscalers and large colos do not buy rack power in Central Europe — they call off against global, multi-year framework agreements negotiated at HQ with Schneider, Vertiv, Eaton and Legrand. Vertiv's own partner documentation names Equinix, Alibaba, AT&T and Telefonica as direct major customers. A CE entrant in its first 12 months cannot intercept those frameworks locally: vendor qualification cycles at this buyer class run 18–36 months and are gated by approved-vendor lists that open only at framework renewal. The 0.40 buyer cell the whole GTM rests on is not 'hard to win' — for the model's Year-1 window it is contractually unavailable, and the funnel silently treats it as reachable spend.","category":"competitive","targetNodes":["cust.operator-large"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Large operators procure rack PDUs through global/annual framework agreements with the incumbent top vendors — Vertiv's major-customer list (Equinix, Alibaba, AT&T, Telefonica) documents direct HQ-level supply relationships that CE regional teams call off against rather than re-tender","refs":["digest:cust-mix#c9","digest:cr3-vendors#c6","ledger:cust.operator-large"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"A pre-launch entrant arrives mid-framework: approved-vendor-list slots do not open within its 12-month horizon, and no source in the corpus documents any local discretionary rack-PDU spend at large operators outside frameworks — the channel-structure data is entirely absent, so nothing in the model gates this cell by contract calendar","refs":["profile:Stage","digest:cust-mix#c3","ledger:cust.operator-large"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The 0.40 cust.operator-large share flows through SAM/YAM as winnable at the conservative-ramp pace, but the correct treatment for Year 1 is foreclosure: the cell exits the venture's reachable scope entirely, and the YAM built on 'large operators first' is drawn from spend the venture cannot bid on","refs":["ledger:cust.operator-large","ledger:obtainableFactor","profile:followups.primary-buyer"]}],"whyMissable":"The ledger expresses buyer mix as shares of spend with sensitivity bands — a share looks winnable at any pace, so the team reads 0.40 ± band as market structure, not as a contract calendar. The digest confirms zero sources publish contract-structure data for this channel, so nothing in the evidence base ever forced the question 'is this cell open to us at all in Year 1?'","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"The venture (or any comparable new entrant) gets onto a hyperscaler's or large CE colo's approved-vendor list and receives a rack-PDU purchase order within a 12-month window — or documented evidence that CE site teams hold local discretionary PDU budgets outside global frameworks.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"Direct global supply relationships between top PDU vendors and named large operators are documented (Vertiv partner materials); framework/AVL procurement is the standard mechanism at this buyer class, though some operators do run open regional RFQs for second-sourcing","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.operator-large","op":"exclude","value":null,"note":"Large-operator cell foreclosed by framework agreements for the Year-1 window — removed from reachable scope, not merely shrunk"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Vendor-qualification prerequisites in large-operator RFQs (existing global supply agreement or AVL membership required to bid)","where":"Equinix / Digital Realty / NTT EMEA supplier-onboarding portals and CE colo fit-out tender documents","threshold":"≥3 of first 5 large-operator opportunities disqualify at vendor-list stage before any technical evaluation","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Incumbent commentary on multi-year hyperscale/colo supply agreements and backlog for rack power","where":"Schneider Electric and Vertiv quarterly earnings calls, order-backlog and framework-win discussion","threshold":"Explicit reference to multi-year rack-power supply commitments covering European capacity additions","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Interview 3 CE large-operator procurement leads specifically on framework end-dates, second-source policies and local discretionary spend — establishing whether and when the cell opens","type":"information","voiNodeId":"cust.operator-large","note":"Converts the buyer-mix node's biggest cell from 'assumed reachable' to a dated market-entry calendar"},{"action":"Re-sequence GTM to mid/enterprise operators and integrators for Year 1, treating large operators as a Year-2/3 qualification track timed to framework renewals","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Aligns the ramp with the cells that are actually open mid-cycle"},{"action":"Pursue formal second-source qualification slots, which many framework agreements reserve for supply-resilience reasons","type":"contractual","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Second-source status is the only contractual door into a framework mid-term"}]},{"id":"distributor-line-review-lockout","title":"Annual line reviews and exclusivity terms lock the distribution channel for the launch year","narrative":"The distributor cell (13%) and much of the mid/enterprise cell (25%) are reached through master distributors and reseller networks — the MaxPower model, where the distributor sells to resellers, not end customers. Those distributors run annual line reviews: category slots are allocated once a year, often with exclusivity or MDF commitments to the incumbent PDU line, and intelligent-PDU buyer guides note distributors already carry OEM/private-label options that fill the 'value' slot. A pre-launch entrant arrives mid-cycle into a category that is already filled twice over. The result is not a lower win rate — it is that the indirect channel produces zero Year-1 revenue while the model quietly assumes it contributes at the same 3% pace as everything else.","category":"execution","targetNodes":["cust.distributor","obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"CE power-infrastructure distribution operates on the master-distributor model with annual line reviews; category slots for rack power are held by incumbent lines and supplemented by private-label ODM options the distributor already controls","refs":["digest:cust-mix#c8","digest:cust-mix#c13","ledger:cust.distributor"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"An entrant launching mid-cycle cannot obtain a line-card slot until the next review, and no corpus source quantifies indirect share or documents off-cycle onboarding — the mid/enterprise cell that buys through these resellers is similarly delayed, pushing all distribution-routed deals beyond the 12-month window","refs":["profile:Stage","digest:cust-mix#c4","ledger:cust.operator-mid"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"cust.distributor (0.13) is unavailable for Year 1 and the blended Year-1 pace across remaining cells falls below the 3% mid-band, because the funnel applies one obtainableFactor uniformly across channels with radically different onboarding clocks","refs":["ledger:cust.distributor","ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"Distribution looks like the easy channel — low share (0.13), low stakes, 'we'll sign a distributor when ready.' The kill detail is timing, not access: the ledger has no time dimension on buyer cells, so a channel that is open in month 14 and one that is open in month 1 look identical in the funnel.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A named CE power/IT-infrastructure distributor signs the venture's line off-cycle within 6 months of launch without exclusivity conflict — or documented evidence that CE distributors onboard new PDU vendors on rolling rather than annual review calendars.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"Annual line reviews with category exclusivity are standard practice in electrical/IT distribution (base rate of the channel model), but some challenger distributors deliberately onboard new lines off-cycle to differentiate","basis":"base-rate"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.distributor","op":"exclude","value":null,"note":"Distributor cell closed until next line-review cycle — outside the Year-1 window"},{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"scale","value":0.7,"note":"Distribution-routed mid-market deals slip beyond Year 1, dragging the blended ramp below the 3% mid-band"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Rack-power category composition on published line cards of the top-5 CE power/IT-infrastructure distributors","where":"Distributor line cards and published price lists (vendor pages of major CE electrical and IT distribution groups)","threshold":"Category shows incumbent line + a private-label option in ≥4 of 5 line cards — both slots filled","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Response pattern to the venture's distribution inquiries","where":"Venture's own channel-recruitment log across first 2 quarters","threshold":"≥3 distributors respond 'category filled, revisit at next annual review'","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Map the line-review calendars, exclusivity terms and private-label arrangements of the top-5 CE distributors before committing the channel plan","type":"information","voiNodeId":"cust.distributor","note":"Replaces an unevidenced 0.13 cell with a dated channel-opening schedule"},{"action":"Bridge Year 1 with a direct/rep-model sales motion and e-commerce for the mid-market, deferring distribution to the first open review cycle","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":null},{"action":"Offer a non-exclusive, high-margin category slot to a challenger distributor seeking differentiation from the incumbent-locked majors","type":"contractual","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Margin, not volume, is the currency that opens a mid-cycle slot"}]},{"id":"oem-cell-odm-price-foreclosure","title":"The OEM/integrator cell is already owned by Asian ODM white-label economics a CE industrial entrant cannot match","narrative":"The model books 22% of PDU spend to the OEM/integrator channel as if it were open territory. But the corpus shows that channel is precisely where Taiwanese and Chinese ODMs (Digipower, GETEKnet and peers) live: rack integrators source intelligent PDUs white-label at ODM cost points, with private-label flexibility built into the sourcing terms. A Central European industrial entrant with its own manufacturing base competes in that cell on the one dimension where it is structurally weakest — landed unit cost at integrator volumes. This is not a bake-off it can lose; it is a bake-off it will never be invited to, because integrator RFQs are decided on BOM cost before any intelligence feature is evaluated. The 0.22 cell inflates SAM with spend that is priced out of reach on day one.","category":"competitive","targetNodes":["cust.oem"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The OEM/integrator channel for intelligent PDUs is served by established ODM white-label suppliers — Digipower supports global OEM partners from Taiwan, GETEKnet markets explicitly as an OEM rack-PDU supplier, and buyer guides list OEM/private-label options as standard distributor sourcing terms","refs":["digest:cust-mix#c11","digest:cust-mix#c12","digest:cust-mix#c13"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Integrator purchasing is decided on landed BOM cost and private-label flexibility at volume; a pre-launch CE industrial manufacturer cannot reach ODM price points, and no corpus source quantifies OEM/ODM share precisely because the channel is opaque, commodity-priced, and relationship-locked","refs":["profile:Product","profile:Company","ledger:cust.oem"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The 0.22 cust.oem cell is structurally foreclosed by cost position rather than contested — carrying it in SAM overstates the venture's serviceable spend, and any GTM plan that counts integrator volume in the ramp is counting revenue from a channel that never issues it an RFQ","refs":["ledger:cust.oem","ledger:serviceableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"The cell carries the label 'OEM/integrator' — which to an industrial manufacturer reads as its natural home turf, not enemy territory. The surface appearance is affinity ('we are a manufacturer, OEMs are our people'); the hidden reality is that in this channel the venture is the customer's competitor's customer: integrators buy from ODMs whose cost structure defines the price, and the ledger's zero-evidence 0.22 was never stress-tested against that.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Quote-level comparison showing the venture can land within ~10–15% of ODM white-label pricing at integrator volumes, or a rack-integrator design win awarded on features/compliance rather than unit price.","likelihood":{"value":0.5,"rationale":"The ODM white-label channel for intelligent PDUs is directly documented in the corpus (Digipower, GETEKnet, private-label sourcing terms), and industrial-entrant cost disadvantage vs Asian ODM at volume is a robust pattern; some integrators do pay premiums for EU-origin and short lead times","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.oem","op":"exclude","value":null,"note":"OEM/integrator cell foreclosed by ODM cost structure — unavailable to a CE industrial entrant, not merely competitive"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"ASP gap between ODM-listed intelligent PDUs and the venture's target price at integrator volumes","where":"ODM published price lists and B2B marketplace listings (Digipower, GETEKnet, Alibaba-listed smart-PDU lines) vs the venture's cost model","threshold":"ODM intelligent-PDU ASPs ≥30% below the venture's achievable price at 500+ unit volumes","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Basis-of-award in rack-integrator RFQs (price vs features/origin/compliance)","where":"Integrator RFQ responses and OCP marketplace/integrated-rack product listings specifying PDU sourcing","threshold":"≥2 of first 3 integrator RFQs decided on unit price with white-label requirement","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission a teardown and landed-cost benchmark of 3 leading ODM intelligent PDUs against the venture's BOM before building any OEM-channel revenue into the plan","type":"information","voiNodeId":"cust.oem","note":"Directly tests whether the 0.22 cell is priced in or out of reach"},{"action":"Reposition for the OEM channel only where EU-manufacture matters: compliance-sensitive, short-lead-time, and sovereignty-driven builds — conceding commodity white-label volume","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":null},{"action":"Explore becoming the EU-based second source for one ODM-dependent integrator facing supply-chain or customs-origin pressure","type":"contractual","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Import dependence creates a narrow contractual wedge into an otherwise closed cell"}]},{"id":"bundling-eliminates-bakeoffs-year1","title":"Incumbent power-train bundling means the standalone PDU bake-off the 3% ramp assumes rarely happens","narrative":"The 3% Year-1 obtainable factor implicitly assumes the venture gets evaluated: a buyer runs a PDU selection, the venture competes, and wins its share. But the growth capacity the 9% CAGR is made of — new builds and major fit-outs — buys rack PDUs inside integrated packages from Schneider, Vertiv, Eaton and Legrand, who can price the PDU line at near-zero margin inside busway + rack + UPS + cooling bundles worth orders of magnitude more. In those deals there is no PDU bake-off to enter. The corpus's own evidence already screams this: the generic 1–5% band is contradicted by every concrete datapoint found — a worked example at ~0.1% of SAM, and a real industrial-hardware entrant (Tractian) starting at ~0.001% of its market. The 3% mid-band survived because it was framed as 'already revised down from 6%' — a conservatism that is only conservative against fantasy.","category":"competitive","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The five to seven vendors named as market leaders (Schneider, Eaton, ABB, Legrand, Vertiv, Siemens, Tripp Lite) all sell PDUs as one line inside a ~$23B power-equipment layer of integrated DC fit-out packages, enabling near-zero-margin PDU pricing within bundles","refs":["digest:cr3-vendors#c6","digest:cust-mix#c2","ledger:shape.cr3"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"New-build and fit-out capacity — where market growth concentrates — is specified as a package, so standalone PDU evaluations exist mainly in retrofit/replacement sockets; every concrete Year-1 datapoint in the corpus (TryBuildCo ~0.1% of SAM, Tractian ~$200K against a $14.9B market) lands one to three orders of magnitude below the generic 1–5% band, and one source frames SOM as a 3–5 year window, not Year 1","refs":["ledger:shape.cagr","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c5","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c6","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c4"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"obtainableFactor 0.03 assumes access to competitive evaluations across the whole SAM; restricted to the standalone-evaluation slice with hardware-entrant win rates, the realistic Year-1 pace is ≤0.5% of SAM, cutting YAM by roughly five-sixths","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c8"]}],"whyMissable":"The 3% carries the halo of a downward revision (from 6%) and sits mid-band of two cited benchmarks — it looks like the conservative choice that has already been challenged once. What hides the risk is that both benchmarks are generic startup heuristics; the sector-specific question 'in what fraction of CE PDU spend does a standalone evaluation even occur?' is never asked by a percentage-of-SAM framing.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"In the first two quarters, ≥2 CE opportunities at operators reach a standalone PDU bake-off (venture invited, incumbent bundle displaced or absent) — or a bottom-up pipeline model (reps × win-rate × cycle time) that independently supports ≥3% of SAM in 12 months.","likelihood":{"value":0.55,"rationale":"The contradiction is already documented in the corpus: every concrete observed Year-1 datapoint sits far below the 1–5% band, and bundle-selling by integrated power vendors is standard; uncertainty remains on how much CE retrofit demand buys PDUs standalone","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"set","value":0.005,"note":"Year-1 pace re-anchored to observed hardware-entrant base rates on the standalone-evaluation slice, not the generic 1–5% heuristic"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Fraction of addressable Year-1 opportunities where rack PDUs are specified standalone vs inside an integrated fit-out package","where":"CE colo/enterprise fit-out tender documents and the venture's CRM opportunity coding","threshold":"<30% of qualified opportunities are standalone PDU purchases","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Incumbent commentary on integrated-solution attach rates and rack-system bundling","where":"Vertiv and Schneider quarterly earnings calls; product-launch materials for integrated rack/power-train offerings","threshold":"Explicit strategy statements on selling rack power as part of integrated systems, with PDUs positioned as an attach line","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Replace the percentage heuristic with a capacity model built from the first 90 days of CE outreach: standalone-RFQ density × win rate × cycle time × fulfillment capacity","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"The corpus explicitly recommends this method over percentage heuristics; 90 days of pipeline data converts the node from folklore to measurement"},{"action":"Target retrofit and replacement RFQs — where PDUs are bought standalone and bundle pricing has no leverage — as the entire Year-1 focus","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":null},{"action":"Get spec'd as the named alternate PDU in integrator and consulting-engineer BOM templates, so bundled deals carry an entry point","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":null}]},{"id":"legrand-dual-brand-intelligent-pdu-lock","title":"Legrand's dual ownership of Raritan and Server Technology makes the intelligent-PDU sub-market far more closed than the 0.55 whole-market CR3 suggests","narrative":"The ledger's CR3 of 0.55 is extrapolated from whole-PDU-market data and even the digest treats Raritan and Server Technology as separate 'top players' — but both belong to Legrand. In the intelligent-PDU sub-market where this venture actually plays, one owner controls the two pioneering brands whose outlet-level monitoring APIs are wired into operators' DCIM and monitoring stacks, alongside Vertiv (Geist) and Schneider with their own DCIM suites. That changes the nature of the serviceable market: the enterprise (29%) and colocation (18%) replacement sockets that a Year-1 entrant must live off are not vendor-neutral — swapping the PDU means re-integrating the monitoring layer, so incumbents win renewals without a selection event. The 0.55 serviceable factor treats regulatory reach as the only haircut; the software-integration lock quietly removes another tranche of TAM from anyone's reach but the incumbents'.","category":"fact","targetNodes":["serviceableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The CR3 estimate rests on whole-PDU data and a single 15.8% Schneider datapoint, while Credence lists Server Technology and Raritan as separate top players — masking that Legrand owns both intelligent-PDU pioneer brands, so intelligent-PDU concentration is materially higher than the whole-market 0.55 proxy","refs":["digest:cr3-vendors#c5","digest:cr3-vendors#c0","ledger:shape.cr3"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Intelligent PDUs are purchased as endpoints of DCIM/monitoring stacks; installed bases in the enterprise and colocation segments — the venture's realistic Year-1 sockets — are standardized on incumbent APIs and management suites, so replacement demand renews with the incumbent without a competitive evaluation","refs":["ledger:seg.enterprise","ledger:seg.colocation","profile:Product"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The serviceable haircut (0.55) was justified only by the EU certification stack and carries no integration-lock component; adding the DCIM-locked share of replacement demand pushes the truly serviceable fraction below the model's 0.45 low edge","refs":["ledger:serviceableFactor","digest:cr3-vendors#c1"]}],"whyMissable":"Two separate camouflages stack: the vendor lists the team read name Raritan and Server Technology as distinct competitors (so the intelligent-PDU duopoly-under-one-roof never registers), and serviceableFactor was framed as a regulatory/channel haircut, so software-integration lock was never on the list of things that could shrink it. No source publishes sub-market shares — the Omdia table that would reveal this is paywalled — so the whole-market proxy passed unchallenged.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"The Omdia 2025 rack-PDU vendor-share table showing intelligent-PDU sub-segment concentration at or below the whole-market level — or CE operator interviews confirming that open protocols (SNMP/Redfish) make mixing PDU vendors within an existing DCIM stack a routine, low-friction decision.","likelihood":{"value":0.35,"rationale":"Legrand's dual-brand ownership is fact and DCIM integration stickiness is well documented qualitatively, but the actual locked fraction of CE replacement demand is unquantified — no source publishes sub-market shares or switching-cost data, so the magnitude rests on judgment","basis":"judgment"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"serviceableFactor","op":"set","value":0.38,"note":"Regulatory haircut plus DCIM/API integration-locked replacement sockets removed from reachable scope — below the model's 0.45 low edge"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Integration requirements in CE retrofit/replacement RFQs (incumbent-compatible APIs or named DCIM suites specified as mandatory)","where":"CE colocation and enterprise retrofit tender documents;
